title: Japanese GP Winners and Losers: Keisner's Masterstroke, Ferrari's Misstep, and Zwart's Costly Error
meta: Published: 30 March 2026 | By Lena Müller, Strategy Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Robin Keisner celebrates his maiden victory — a result few saw coming.
body:
Let’s rewind to lap 18, turn 14. Jason Roy has just crashed his Red Bull, the safety car is deployed, and the pit wall faces the first major branch point of the Japanese Grand Prix. Ferrari and Racing Bulls dive for the pits, fitting fresh intermediates to Cachinho, Kevu, and Wright. The rest of the field stays out. That single decision — pit under safety car for fresh rubber, or preserve track position — branched the race into two completely different futures. Robin Keisner and Audi read the other branch. They stayed out. They covered every phase that followed. And they won by 2.595 seconds.

The strategic pattern of this race was defined by three offset windows. First, the early safety car that offered a free pit stop to anyone willing to sacrifice track position. Second, a long middle phase where those who had stopped early found themselves buried in a DRS‑less train, unable to make progress as the track dried and their fresh tyres overheated. Third, a late‑race crossover where those on the optimal strategy — long first stint, late stop — were perfectly placed when the final safety car bunched the field. Keisner and Audi navigated all three. The alternatives that haunted the rest of the grid were stark.

Winner: Robin Keisner and Audi

Keisner started 10th, was promoted to 9th by Vandesteene’s grid penalty, and gained 9 positions to win. He now sits 2nd in the championship with 41 points, 23 behind Väisänen. The numbers are impressive. But the real story is the decision tree. When the lap‑18 safety car was deployed, Audi kept both cars on track. Keisner ran long, preserved his intermediates through the drying phase, and pitted at the optimal moment — late enough to emerge ahead of the early stoppers, early enough to clear Cachinho before the final restart. The alternative? Pit under the safety car, like Ferrari and Racing Bulls, and spend the rest of the race stuck in traffic. The cost of that alternative was 25 points lost. Keisner and Audi got it right.

Richard Hicks, Keisner’s teammate, finished 6th — a combined 18 positions gained between the two Audi drivers. Audi now sit 2nd in the constructors’ championship with 50 points, 14 behind Ferrari. For a manufacturer in its first full season, that is not progress. It is a warning.

Winner: Eren Aygen and Williams

Aygen qualified 8th, started 7th after the Vandesteene penalty, and finished 2nd — a drive that moves him to 3rd in the championship with 34 points. His overtake on Vandesteene for P3 in the closing laps was decisive: a sweep around the outside that required absolute commitment. The Williams looked balanced in the high‑speed corners, the Mercedes engine upgrade providing a detectable straight‑line advantage. But the bigger Williams story is the absence of Lucas Koopman, who crashed on lap 1 and will miss at least three races with a back injury. Hamish Porter, currently 2nd in the F2 standings, has been called up. The cost to Williams’ constructors’ push is uncertain, but Aygen’s podium has softened the blow.

Winner: Sarah Springfield and Aston Martin

Springfield ran as high as 2nd and finished 5th, collecting 10 points that represent her fourth consecutive points finish. She has now scored all 24 of Aston Martin’s points this season. Her race was built on an aggressive long‑first‑stint strategy, staying out when Mercedes pitted, hoping for a safety car or VSC that arrived too late to benefit from. The frustration was evident: “We were staying out hoping for a VSC or a Safety Car but it just came too late for us to benefit from.” Yet a P5 from P6 on the grid, in a car that has no right to be consistently in the top six, is a strategy result disguised as disappointment. What if the safety car had come five laps earlier? Springfield could have been on the podium. The window was that close.

Loser: Ferrari’s Strategy — and the Championship Lead That Shrank

Väisänen started from pole and finished 7th. Cachinho started last, pitted early under the safety car, and spent the rest of the race as a mobile roadblock on dying tyres, finishing 13th. Ferrari’s decision to pit Cachinho under the lap‑18 safety car was the branch point that cost them. The logic was clear: fresh intermediates on a drying track, a chance to undercut the field. But the execution failed. Cachinho could not clear the cars ahead, his tyres degraded in the dry line, and he became a rolling chicane that actually helped Keisner by holding up the chasing pack after the final restart. Väisänen, who stayed out, was caught between the early stoppers and the long‑runners, his own strategy neutralised. The combined result: 7 points from a race where Ferrari had pole position. The championship lead shrank from 30 points to 23. The what‑if is brutal: if Ferrari had kept both cars out, Väisänen would have retained track position and likely finished on the podium. The alternative cost was 15 points, perhaps more.

Loser: Maikel Zwart and Joshua Nathanial — A Collision That Cost Both

Zwart led 20 laps of this race. He was in control of the restart, held off Nathanial at turn one, and was managing his intermediates beautifully. Then Mercedes gambled on an ultra‑long first stint, leaving him out until lap 38 while his teammate Vandesteene — on fresh tyres — was lapping three seconds faster. By the time Zwart pitted, he had surrendered the undercut and emerged behind Keisner. Pushing too hard on cold tyres to recover, he misjudged his braking and slammed into the back of Nathanial’s McLaren. Both cars out. Both drivers scored zero. Nathanial, who had driven his best race of the season and was on course for a podium, remains without a point after four rounds. Zwart sits 11th with 12 points. The collision was avoidable, and the strategy that preceded it — waiting too long to pit — created the conditions for the error. A double DNF that cost Mercedes a probable 33 points.

Loser: Red Bull’s Disappearing Act

Jason Roy crashed out on lap 18 — a mistake under braking that nearly collected Sam Fakt. Philippe Verhaeren finished 9th, collecting 2 points. Red Bull have now scored 25 points as a team, placing them 7th in the constructors’ championship, behind Williams and Alpine. Roy qualified 17th, his worst session of the season, and has not scored since his China podium. The car that looked quickest over one lap in Shanghai has vanished in the rain. The question for the pit wall: is this a wet‑weather weakness, or a deeper problem?

The Phase That Defined the Race

The lap‑18 safety car was the moment the race branched. Ferrari and Racing Bulls took the early pit stop. Mercedes, Audi, Williams, and Aston Martin stayed out. Those who stayed out filled the top six positions. Those who pitted early finished 7th, 10th, 13th, and outside the points. The phase was decisive, and the teams that read it correctly gained an average of 8.4 positions per car. The teams that misread it lost an average of 6.2. In a wet race where overtaking was difficult and DRS was unavailable for long stretches, track position was everything. The pit wall that understood that won the race. Now, here’s the question that will haunt the flight to Bahrain: what if Ferrari had simply left both cars on track? The championship might look very different.
